summary: The tech spec demonstrates reasonable security awareness — HKCU-only registry changes, per-app firewall rules, in-memory-only traffic processing, and local-only CA key generation are all sound design choices. However, several significant gaps remain that must be addressed before implementation begins. The most critical issue is the unconditional stripping of Content-Security-Policy headers on every response, which transforms the proxy into an active XSS amplifier for the entire browser session. Additional major concerns include: the state file has no integrity protection, the proxy binds on 0.0.0.0 by default in mitmproxy (needs explicit 127.0.0.1 binding), the VPN health check calls an external IP-geo API that is itself a privacy leak, CLI country-code input is not validated before use in preset lookups, and no crash-recovery cleanup is run on --stop when stale state is detected. These issues are fixable in implementation without redesign.

findings.description: The spec mandates stripping Content-Security-Policy headers from all HTML responses so that injected JS executes without being blocked. This is the broadest possible approach: every page the user visits during an active session loses its XSS, clickjacking, and mixed-content protections. If the user visits a banking site, corporate intranet, or any site with a meaningful CSP while geo-fix is running, those protections are silently gone. A malicious ad or third-party script on any page can now exfiltrate session cookies or perform actions the CSP was designed to prevent. The spec does not limit CSP stripping to target domains (google.com) or to responses that actually need JS injection.
findings.fix: Strip CSP only on responses where JS injection is actually being performed, and only on the specific headers that block inline scripts (script-src, default-src). Preserve frame-ancestors, form-action, upgrade-insecure-requests, and all other directives. Better yet, instead of stripping, add a nonce to both the CSP script-src directive and the injected <script> tag so the page CSP remains intact for all other scripts. Limit this mitigation exclusively to the domains that need spoofing (*.google.com) rather than all traffic.

findings.description: The spec describes the proxy as running on ':8080' (architecture diagram) and mitmproxy's default listen address is 0.0.0.0 unless explicitly overridden. If the machine is on a LAN (home network, office, cafe WiFi), any other device on that network can route traffic through the user's mitmproxy instance. Because the proxy holds the CA private key and performs full TLS termination, a remote attacker on the same LAN could use it as a man-in-the-middle proxy for their own traffic — and the user's CA cert would sign it. This would also allow a remote party to probe or enumerate services through the proxy.
findings.fix: Explicitly bind mitmproxy to 127.0.0.1:8080, not 0.0.0.0. In mitmproxy options: options.listen_host = '127.0.0.1'. Add a startup assertion that verifies the bound address. Document this in the security section of the spec.

findings.category: CA Certificate Handling — No Key-at-Rest Protection
findings.description: The mitmproxy CA private key is stored in ~/.mitmproxy/mitmproxy-ca.pem (plaintext PEM) with default file permissions set by mitmproxy — typically 0600 on Linux but world-readable on Windows unless explicitly restricted. Any process running as the same Windows user (malware, another application) can read this key and use it to issue trusted certificates for any domain, enabling silent MITM attacks against the user. The spec notes 'the key exists only on the user's machine' but does not specify that its permissions are hardened.
findings.fix: After CA generation, explicitly set the key file ACL to be readable only by the current user account (using icacls or Python's os.chmod equivalent on Windows). Consider storing the key in the Windows Credential Store or DPAPI-encrypted instead of plaintext. Add a startup check that warns if the key file permissions are too permissive. Document the key location and what 'compromise' means in the user README.

findings.category: State File Security — No Integrity Verification
findings.description: The spec states a state file tracks active configuration for crash recovery: 'On next launch, if stale state detected, cleanup runs first.' If a malicious actor (or compromised process running as the same user) can write to this state file, they could inject arbitrary proxy settings — for example pointing the proxy address to a remote host, or altering the country preset to inject attacker-controlled coordinates or language headers. The spec does not describe the state file format, location, or any integrity mechanism.
findings.fix: Store the state file in a location writable only by the application (e.g., a temp directory with restricted ACLs, or alongside the exe). Sign or HMAC the state file contents using a key derived from the installation path or a machine-specific value (DPAPI). At minimum, validate all fields against a strict schema before acting on them — reject any value not in the known-good preset list. Do not store the proxy address in the state file; derive it from the hardcoded 127.0.0.1:8080 constant.

findings.category: VPN Health Check — Privacy Leak to External IP-Geo API
findings.description: The spec says health_check.py verifies VPN status by checking public IP against 'known Russian IP ranges or simply checking if IP geo differs from RU' using the requests library. This call goes out to a third-party IP geolocation service (e.g., ipapi.co, ip-api.com, or similar). This means: (1) the external service learns the user's real IP before the VPN check completes, (2) if the call fails (API down, rate-limited), the app may give a false 'VPN OK' signal, (3) the service sees a pattern of repeated lookups that could fingerprint the user. The spec does not name the service, so its data retention practices are unknown.
findings.fix: Prefer a local check: compare the system's default gateway or network adapter DNS suffix against known patterns, or check if a VPN adapter (tun/tap interface) is present using Windows WMI/netsh. If an external call is necessary, use a minimal endpoint (e.g., a plain-text IP echo like https://api.ipify.org) rather than a full geo-API, and perform the lookup through the VPN tunnel (after the proxy starts). Implement a timeout and fail-safe: if the check is inconclusive, warn but do not block. Never log or store the returned IP.

findings.category: Input Validation — CLI Country Code Not Validated
findings.description: The CLI accepts a country code argument (geo-fix.exe US) which is described as being passed to preset lookup. The spec does not describe validation of this input before use. If the country code is used as a dictionary key into presets.py without sanitization, an attacker who can control the command line (e.g., via a malicious .lnk shortcut or shell injection in a script) could potentially pass unexpected values. More concretely, if the preset lookup failure is not handled gracefully, the app may crash leaving proxy settings in an applied but non-functional state (proxy set, mitmproxy not running — all traffic blocked).
findings.fix: Validate country code input strictly: uppercase, 2 ASCII letters only, must exist in the PRESETS dictionary. Reject and exit with a clear error message on any other value. Perform this validation as the very first step in main.py before any system changes are made. Add a unit test for invalid country codes.

findings.category: Cleanup on Crash — Incomplete Crash Recovery Path
findings.description: The spec describes a state file that enables cleanup on next launch, but the --stop path is the only explicit cleanup trigger described. If the process is killed (task manager, BSOD, power loss) while the proxy is running, the WinINET registry proxy setting remains pointing to 127.0.0.1:8080 where nothing is listening. This means all browser HTTP/HTTPS traffic silently fails until either the user manually removes the proxy setting or geo-fix is relaunched and detects stale state. For a non-technical user, this looks like 'the internet is broken.' The spec also does not describe what happens if mitmproxy crashes but the main process continues running.
findings.fix: Register an atexit handler and a Windows structured exception handler to run cleanup synchronously on crash. Additionally, write the stale-state detection check not just 'on next launch' but also expose a standalone geo-fix.exe --cleanup command that can be embedded in a recovery shortcut. Consider using a Windows Job Object to tie the mitmproxy subprocess lifetime to the parent process so mitmproxy cannot outlive main.py. Document in the README that if the internet stops working, the user should run the stop shortcut.

findings.category: Registry Modification — HKCU Scope Is Correct but Revert Needs Atomicity
findings.description: The spec correctly limits registry changes to HKCU (current user, no system-wide impact). However, the revert-on-shutdown procedure is described informally. If the app reads the original proxy value at startup, stores it in memory, then restores it on shutdown, a crash between startup and state-file-write means the original value is lost and cannot be restored. If the user had a legitimate proxy configured before running geo-fix, that setting would be permanently overwritten.
findings.fix: At startup, before modifying any registry key, write the original value to the state file on disk (not just in memory). Use a write-then-rename pattern (atomic file replacement) to ensure the state file is never partially written. On cleanup, restore the original value from the state file. If the state file is missing (first run), treat the original value as 'no proxy' but still verify this assumption.

findings.category: Firewall Rules — Rule Name Collision and Persistence After Uninstall
findings.description: The spec describes creating per-app firewall rules for chrome.exe and msedge.exe via netsh. It does not specify rule naming conventions or whether rules are removed on --stop or only on uninstall. If the rule names are generic (e.g., 'Block WebRTC'), re-running setup could create duplicate rules. More importantly, if the user uninstalls the tool without running --stop or a dedicated uninstaller, the firewall rules persist permanently — blocking WebRTC for Chrome and Edge even without geo-fix running.
findings.fix: Use a consistent, unique naming prefix for all created rules (e.g., 'geo-fix-webrtc-chrome-udp-out'). On --stop, remove the firewall rules (not just the proxy settings). Track created rule names in the state file. Provide a standalone --uninstall command that removes all rules, certs, and state files. In the README, include manual cleanup instructions in case --uninstall cannot be run.

findings.category: Injected JS — Malicious HTML in Proxy Responses
findings.description: The proxy_addon injects JS by string-manipulating HTML response bodies (inserting a <script> tag into <head>). If a response body is malformed, truncated, or contains unusual encoding, the injection could produce broken HTML or — in pathological cases — allow content injection in unexpected positions. The spec mentions handling gzip/brotli and partial HTML but does not describe how injection handles edge cases like: <head> appearing inside a comment or CDATA, multiple <head> tags, or responses where <head> is split across mitmproxy flow chunks.
findings.fix: Use a proper HTML parser (html.parser or lxml) rather than regex/string search to locate the injection point. If no <head> tag is found, inject at the very beginning of the response. Set a hard size limit on responses eligible for injection (e.g., skip responses > 5MB or with content-type not matching text/html). Validate that the injected <script> tag content is static and does not incorporate any request-derived values.

findings.category: OWASP A05 — Security Misconfiguration: CSP Stripping as Default Behavior
findings.description: Stripping CSP on all HTML responses is a system-wide security misconfiguration in OWASP terms. The proxy effectively downgrades the security posture of every website the user visits, not just the spoofed target. This is a form of A05:2021 Security Misconfiguration where a protective control (CSP) is disabled globally. This also has implications under A03:2021 Injection — without CSP, injected content from any source (ads, compromised CDNs) has full DOM access.
findings.fix: See the critical finding on CSP removal above. Apply the principle of least privilege: only disable the minimum necessary CSP directives, only on target domains, only when injection is actually performed.

findings.category: OWASP A09 — Security Logging and Monitoring: No Audit Trail
findings.description: The spec explicitly states 'no request/response data is stored to disk' and describes no logging at all. While avoiding traffic logs is correct for privacy, the absence of any audit logging means there is no way to detect if the proxy was misused, if an attacker accessed it from the LAN (see critical finding), or if the CA key was used to issue rogue certificates. For a personal tool this is acceptable, but the spec should explicitly distinguish between traffic logging (prohibited) and security-event logging (recommended).
findings.fix: Add a minimal security event log (not traffic log) that records: proxy start/stop timestamps, CA cert generation date, number of active connections, and any connection attempts from non-localhost addresses. Store this log in a rotating file with a 7-day retention window. This log should contain no URL paths, headers, or body content.
